headline: Ghosts of Frankfurt: Czechia Seeks Revenge Against Korean Warriors
subheadline: Twenty years after a stunning tournament defeat, the Czechs return to settle old scores in a battle of European grit versus Asian flair.
openingNarrative:
The ghosts of Frankfurt still haunt Czech football. On June 22, 2006, in a sterile stadium in western Germany, South Korea delivered a 2-0 knockout punch that sent a Czech Republic side ranked second in the world tumbling out of the tournament at the group stage. It was supposed to be their golden moment—Pavel Nedvěd, Tomáš Rosický, and Jan Koller leading a generation that had reached the Euro 2004 semifinals to football's ultimate prize. Instead, the Taegeuk Warriors danced on their dreams.

Now, twenty years later, these two nations meet again on the tournament stage, both carrying the weight of different expectations. Czechia—competing under their updated name for the first time at a tournament—arrive as the underdogs who clawed their way here through playoff drama. Miroslav Koubek's appointment in October 2025 after Ivan Hašek's dismissal sparked a remarkable transformation. Penalty shootout victories over Wales and Iceland in the playoff rounds weren't just qualification—they were catharsis for a nation that had watched six tournaments pass them by.

South Korea, meanwhile, march into their eleventh consecutive tournament appearance as Asia's standard-bearers, led by the ageless brilliance of Son Heung-min. At 33 and now plying his trade with LAFC after a record MLS transfer, the Tottenham legend knows this may be his final dance on football's biggest stage. The pressure is immense: Korea's 2002 semifinal run at home remains the continent's greatest tournament achievement, a benchmark that has both inspired and haunted every Korean squad since.

tacticalBreakdown.title: The Chess Match
tacticalBreakdown.narrative:
This match presents a fascinating study in contrasts. Czechia's newly-minted 3-5-2 system under Koubek prioritizes defensive solidity and direct transitions, with Tomáš Souček—stripped of his captaincy but not his importance—anchoring the midfield as the fulcrum between defense and attack. The Czech approach is built on collective discipline: three center-backs providing aerial dominance, wing-backs offering width, and the Souček-Kral partnership screening everything in front of their back line. When they win possession, they look to release Adam Hlozek and Patrik Schick quickly, bypassing Korean pressure through direct balls.

Hong Myung-bo's South Korea presents a more fluid proposition in their 4-3-3, though the coach continues to tinker between back-four and back-three systems. The Korean approach revolves around Son Heung-min's movement across the front line and Lee Kang-in's creative distribution from deeper positions. Their pressing intensity aims to suffocate Czech build-up play before it can develop rhythm, forcing turnovers in dangerous areas. The key tactical battle will unfold in midfield, where Souček's physicality and late runs must be balanced against Korea's quicker, more technical triangle. If Korea can isolate the aging Czech defense against Son's pace in transition moments, they could recreate their 2006 magic.
tacticalBreakdown.keyMatchup: Souček's box-to-box presence versus Korea's midfield press and Son's movement

dataInsights.title: By The Numbers
dataInsights.eloAnalysis: Czechia's ELO rating of 1700 (18th globally) edges South Korea's 1655 (24th), reflecting the Europeans' recent playoff heroics and home continent bias in the rankings.
dataInsights.formAnalysis: Czech qualifying was patchy (5W-3D-2L) but they peaked when it mattered most, while Korea's perfect 6W-4D-0L qualifying record was dampened by recent friendly losses to Ivory Coast and Austria.
dataInsights.squadValueComparison: Korea's €315M squad value significantly exceeds Czechia's €250M, driven primarily by Kim Min-jae's €87.5M valuation at Bayern Munich versus Adam Hlozek's €30M as the Czech standard-bearer.
dataInsights.injuryImpact: Both sides carry concerns over key attackers—Hlozek's recurring calf issues mirror Schick's fitness battles, while Son's workload management at age 33 could limit his impact in the tournament's opening stages.
dataInsights.bettingPerspective: With no clear tournament winner odds available for either side, this reflects their status as respected but unfancied nations capable of surprising bigger names but unlikely to lift the trophy.
dataInsights.historicalPattern: As representatives of UEFA and AFC respectively, both teams face the harsh reality that only European and South American nations have ever won the tournament—Korea's 2002 semifinal remains Asia's high-water mark.

playerSpotlights.name: Son Heung-min
playerSpotlights.narrative: At 33, the LAFC superstar carries the weight of a nation's dreams on his shoulders. His 136 caps tell the story of a player who has defined Korean football for over a decade, but this tournament feels like a final chapter rather than a new beginning. The $26.5M MLS transfer last August was meant to preserve his body for this moment, managing the wear and tear that comes with being Korea's talisman. His movement between the lines, cutting inside from the left or dropping deep to create, remains Korea's most potent weapon. The foot issues that have plagued him throughout qualifying are being carefully managed, but when the moment arrives, expect Son to produce the magic that has made him Asia's greatest export.
playerSpotlights.statline: Over 40 international goals, most by any current Asian player

playerSpotlights.name: Tomáš Souček
playerSpotlights.narrative: The West Ham warrior embodies everything about this Czech renaissance—gritty, determined, and arriving at the crucial moment. Stripped of his captaincy when Koubek took charge, Souček could have sulked. Instead, he doubled down on what makes him indispensable: those thunderous late runs into the box, the aerial dominance, the relentless pressing that breaks up opposition attacks. His partnership with club teammate Vladimír Coufal remains one of international football's most effective combinations. Against Korea's technical midfield, Souček's physicality and tactical intelligence will be crucial in disrupting their rhythm and providing the platform for Czech counter-attacks.
playerSpotlights.statline: Box-to-box midfielder who scored 4 goals in Czech qualifying campaign

playerSpotlights.name: Kim Min-jae
playerSpotlights.narrative: The Bayern Munich center-back represents the modern Korean defender—technically excellent, physically imposing, and comfortable bringing the ball out from deep positions. His €87.5M valuation reflects his status as one of world football's premier center-backs, a player who has mastered the art of defensive leadership in European football's most demanding league. Against Czech physicality, Kim's ability to win aerial duels and organize the defensive line will be crucial. His ball-playing skills could also provide Korea with an additional creative outlet, bypassing Czech pressing through accurate long passes to Son and Lee Kang-in in advanced positions.
playerSpotlights.statline: World-class center-back anchoring Bayern Munich's defense

playerSpotlights.name: Adam Hlozek
playerSpotlights.narrative: The Hoffenheim forward represents Czech football's future, a versatile attacker whose development in the Bundesliga has added crucial quality to Koubek's front line. His 4 goals in qualifying made him the Czech's leading scorer, but recurring calf problems have made his fitness a constant concern heading into the tournament. When healthy, Hlozek's movement in the final third and clinical finishing provide Czechia with a dimension they've lacked in previous campaigns. His partnership with the experienced Schick could be the key to unlocking Korean defensive organization, using pace and movement to exploit spaces behind Kim Min-jae's high defensive line.
playerSpotlights.statline: Led Czech qualifying with 4 goals despite recurring injury concerns

theVerdict.title: The Verdict
theVerdict.narrative:
This match feels destined for stalemate, a tactical chess match where neither side can deliver the knockout blow. Czech defensive discipline should contain Korean attacking forays for long periods, but Son Heung-min's individual brilliance makes a clean sheet unlikely. Koubek's three-center-back system will frustrate Korean build-up play, forcing them into longer spells of possession without penetration. When Czechia do win the ball, their direct approach could trouble a Korean defense still adjusting to Hong Myung-bo's tactical variations.

Expect a cagey opening 30 minutes as both sides feel each other out, with the game's decisive moments likely coming from set pieces or individual moments of quality rather than sustained tactical superiority. A draw would suit both teams' tournament ambitions—avoiding defeat in their opener keeps qualification hopes alive in what promises to be a competitive Group A. The ghosts of Frankfurt may have to wait a little longer for their exorcism.

alternativeScenarios.reasoning: Korean pace in transition could exploit Czech defensive vulnerabilities, with Son and Lee Kang-in combining to devastating effect if they catch the aging Czech backline on the counter.
Czech set-piece strength and Souček's aerial threat could provide the decisive moment, with their defensive organization frustrating Korean creativity for the full 90 minutes.

closingLine: In football, as in life, some stories demand their sequel—and after twenty years, both nations are ready to write the next chapter.
